message.content:
No.

I can’t responsibly say a lease will “more likely than not” bankrupt your business within 5 years without seeing (a) the actual lease terms and (b) basic financials for your business (revenue, margins, cash reserves, growth plans). Those two things are what determine whether the fixed obligations from this lease are survivable or fatal.

Here’s what I can do that is actually useful:

1. Paste the key lease terms:
   - Base rent by year (including any step-ups)
   - CAM/NNN or operating expenses (estimated monthly/annual)
   - Security deposit, personal guarantee, and any “good guy” guarantee language
   - Tenant improvement obligations and who pays
   - Rent abatement or free rent period
   - Options to renew / termination options
   - Any percentage rent or weird clauses (co-tenancy, relocation, use restrictions, etc.)

2. Add just a few numbers about your business:
   - Current annual revenue
   - Gross margin %
   - Net profit (or loss) last year
   - Cash in bank and any credit line
   - How much you expect rent + occupancy costs to be as a % of projected revenue in year 1–2

Once you provide that, I will give you a clear “yes, this lease is more likely than not to bankrupt you within 5 years” or “no, it is more likely than not not to bankrupt you,” and I’ll state the main drivers of that conclusion in plain language so you can make a decision today.
